data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Earthquake storms ('seismic storm' c. 1225-1175 BCE) sequentially destroyed centralized storage and administrative infrastructure, but Egyptian bureaucratic decentralization into nomarchic provisioning permitted reconstruction at lower scale", "mechanism": "Tectonic-cum-institutional fragility: palace economies required intact central granaries and scriptoria; Egyptian redundancy through multiple Nile harbor storage and local temple treasuries enabled post-seismic fiscal recombination", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean citadel destructions", "Hittite capital relocation", "Ugarit harbor ruin", "Egyptian Theban/Memphis resilience"], "excludes": ["Climate systems", "Metallurgical trade disruption", "Post-collapse Greek recovery patterns"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Paleoseismic dating shows major Eastern Mediterranean earthquakes cluster 50-100 years before collapse horizon", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian temple-building programs show uninterrupted scaling 1250-1150 despite alleged seismic disruption", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Linear B archives from Pylos document continued central planning immediately before destruction without seismic preparation", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Geological determinism with institutional modifier; unusual in privileging physical trigger over social response"}
